cluster.selection_rule: Each exercise uses a deliberate negative/eccentric phase as the primary loading strategy. Primary phase stays Resilience unless a later implementation creates a Capacity-variant derivative card.
cluster.source_caveat: Purpose.txt was requested but not located in the File Library search results; exercise_card_details_for_llm.md and existing resilience card patterns were used as the source basis.

categories.title: 01-10 — Lower-Body Knee & Hip Eccentric Control
categories.focus: Split-stance, squat, step-down, and frontal-plane lowering patterns for knee, hip, ankle, and trunk ownership.

categories.title: 11-20 — Posterior Chain, Hamstring, Calf & Groin Eccentrics
categories.focus: High-value posterior-chain and lower-leg tissue-capacity work, including hamstring, adductor, calf, soleus, and tibialis negatives.

categories.title: 21-30 — Upper-Body Pull/Push Negatives
categories.focus: Vertical pull, horizontal pull, push-up, dip, pressing, and hand-support negatives for shoulder, elbow, wrist, scapular, and trunk control.

categories.title: 31-40 — Trunk, Bracing, Landing & Deceleration Eccentrics
categories.focus: Anti-extension, controlled lowering, low-amplitude landing absorption, and braking drills that turn eccentric control into athletic deceleration quality.

categories.title: 41-50 — Loaded Tempo Negatives & Machine/Cable Eccentrics
categories.focus: Progressively loaded tempo negatives that bridge tissue resilience with capacity work while preserving clean positions and adequate rest.

cards.name: Slow Eccentric Split Squat
cards.cardSummary: Slow Eccentric Split Squat builds front-leg quadriceps and hip control through a deliberate negative phase. Use it when the coach wants joint ownership, braking control, and local tissue capacity rather than speed or fatigue.
cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Resilience phase after Output and main Capacity work, or as a low-volume accessory after the main pattern. Harder eccentric tissue stress should not be placed before sprinting, jumping, cutting, or high-skill work.
cards.description: Slow Eccentric Split Squat is a slow negative/eccentric drill for front-leg quadriceps and hip control. It uses deliberate lowering, clean alignment, and enough rest to build control and tissue capacity without chasing fatigue.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Slow Eccentric / Isometric Joint Resilience. Coach 4-6 seconds lowering, pain-free range, clean alignment, and enough rest to preserve tissue quality. Specific focus: front-leg quadriceps and hip control.
cards.athleteLanguage: Move slow, own the lowering, and stop before pain or compensation.
cards.whyItWorks: Slow eccentrics create time under tension while the athlete must organize joints, breathing, and posture. For Slow Eccentric Split Squat, the slower lower makes compensations obvious and gives the coach time to adjust range, load, or support before pain or collapse.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Resilience / Slow Eccentric-Isometric Joint Resilience because the goal is tissue tolerance, joint ownership, and braking control after higher-priority speed, power, skill, or strength work.
cards.commonMisuse: Do not treat hard negatives as a daily warm-up or conditioning finisher. Avoid placing heavy hamstring, calf, groin, shoulder, elbow, or knee eccentrics before explosive Output when spring, speed, or skill quality matters.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by reducing range, tempo, load, lever length, instability, impact, or weekly frequency before accepting pain, shaking, breath holding, or position loss.

prerequisites: Pain-free basic pattern for Slow Eccentric Split Squat
Ability to stop the set before alignment breaks
No acute flare-up in the target tissue or joint
movementRequirements.quality_threshold: Stop or regress if alignment, breathing, tempo, range control, or pain-free execution breaks down.

joint_resilience_profile.primary_strategy: Controlled eccentric / negative lowering in pain-free owned range.
joint_resilience_profile.range_emphasis: Use the largest range the athlete can own without compensation.
joint_resilience_profile.progression_trigger: Progress only when target tissue tolerates the dose with clean alignment and normal recovery within 24-48 hours.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Slow Eccentric Split Squat is a slow negative/eccentric drill for front-leg quadriceps and hip control. It uses deliberate lowering, clean alignment, and enough rest to build control and tissue capacity without chasing fatigue.
setup: Set a split stance with ribs stacked and front foot rooted.
Choose a load, range, height, or lever length that allows a smooth negative without pain.
Set the athlete's start position before adding tempo or volume.
execution_steps: Create tension and start from a position the athlete can own.
Lower for 4-6 seconds, lightly touch the target depth, then stand with control.
Keep breathing controlled and avoid bouncing, dropping, twisting, or rushing the last third of the range.
Reset each rep so the next negative starts from the same clean position.
athlete_cues: Move like you could pause anywhere.
Control the down, reset the up.
No pain, no collapse.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the rep if needed, then exhale slowly through the lowering phase.
Do not use breath holding to hide loss of position.
common_faults: Rushing the negative after the first half of the range.
Adding load before the athlete owns tempo and alignment.
Turning a tissue-control drill into a fatigue circuit.
Pushing through pain, joint pinch, or visible collapse.
quality_gate: Tempo stays within the prescribed range.
Target joint line stays clean with no uncontrolled wobble or collapse.
Athlete finishes the set with the same posture and range they started with.
No pain response during the set or unusual next-day flare-up.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, cramping that changes mechanics, numbness, dizziness, or joint pinch.
Tempo cannot be maintained for the planned range.
Breath holding, shaking, or compensation becomes the main strategy.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use hands-on support, a higher target, shorter range, and a 2-3 second lowering tempo. Keep the drill playful and stop well before shaking or pain.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use the same exercise with a modest tempo target, usually 3-4 seconds down. Progress range before load.
scaling.teen: Use after technical or output work, not as a fatigue finisher. Keep reps low and require repeatable alignment.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with the simplest setup and a pain-free range. Add load only after the athlete owns the slow lower and reset.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress by adding range, load, lever length, or tempo one variable at a time. Use longer rest and stop short of form failure.
scaling.older_adult: Prefer support, smaller range, lower load, and slower progressions. Prioritize confidence, breathing, and next-day recovery.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only symptom-free versions with clinician guidance when needed. Avoid breath holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, impact, and positions that provoke pelvic-floor, abdominal, or joint symptoms.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-specific adjustment. Individualize by training age, tissue history, joint symptoms, recovery response, pelvic-floor symptoms when relevant, footwear, surface, and fatigue state.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Tempo Front Squat is a slow negative/eccentric drill for front-rack squat control and quadriceps tissue capacity. It uses deliberate lowering, clean alignment, and enough rest to build control and tissue capacity without chasing fatigue.
setup: Set a secure front rack from pins with a load the athlete can lower slowly.
Choose a load, range, height, or lever length that allows a smooth negative without pain.
Set the athlete's start position before adding tempo or volume.
execution_steps: Create tension and start from a position the athlete can own.
Lower for 4-5 seconds, keep elbows high, and stand only if posture remains repeatable.
Keep breathing controlled and avoid bouncing, dropping, twisting, or rushing the last third of the range.
Reset each rep so the next negative starts from the same clean position.
athlete_cues: Move like you could pause anywhere.
Control the down, reset the up.
No pain, no collapse.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the rep if needed, then exhale slowly through the lowering phase.
Do not use breath holding to hide loss of position.
common_faults: Rushing the negative after the first half of the range.
Adding load before the athlete owns tempo and alignment.
Turning a tissue-control drill into a fatigue circuit.
Pushing through pain, joint pinch, or visible collapse.
quality_gate: Tempo stays within the prescribed range.
Target joint line stays clean with no uncontrolled wobble or collapse.
Athlete finishes the set with the same posture and range they started with.
No pain response during the set or unusual next-day flare-up.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, cramping that changes mechanics, numbness, dizziness, or joint pinch.
Tempo cannot be maintained for the planned range.
Breath holding, shaking, or compensation becomes the main strategy.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use hands-on support, a higher target, shorter range, and a 2-3 second lowering tempo. Keep the drill playful and stop well before shaking or pain.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use the same exercise with a modest tempo target, usually 3-4 seconds down. Progress range before load.
scaling.teen: Use after technical or output work, not as a fatigue finisher. Keep reps low and require repeatable alignment.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with the simplest setup and a pain-free range. Add load only after the athlete owns the slow lower and reset.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress by adding range, load, lever length, or tempo one variable at a time. Use longer rest and stop short of form failure.
scaling.older_adult: Prefer support, smaller range, lower load, and slower progressions. Prioritize confidence, breathing, and next-day recovery.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only symptom-free versions with clinician guidance when needed. Avoid breath holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, impact, and positions that provoke pelvic-floor, abdominal, or joint symptoms.

cards.name: Single-Leg Box Squat Negative

cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Resilience phase after Output and main Capacity work, or as a low-volume accessory after the main pattern. Harder eccentric tissue stress should not be placed before sprinting, jumping, cutting, or high-skill work.
cards.description: Single-Leg Box Squat Negative is a slow negative/eccentric drill for single-leg squat eccentric capacity. It uses deliberate lowering, clean alignment, and enough rest to build control and tissue capacity without chasing fatigue.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Slow Eccentric / Isometric Joint Resilience. Coach 5 seconds lowering, pain-free range, clean alignment, and enough rest to preserve tissue quality. Specific focus: single-leg squat eccentric capacity.
cards.athleteLanguage: Move slow, own the lowering, and stop before pain or compensation.
cards.whyItWorks: Slow eccentrics create time under tension while the athlete must organize joints, breathing, and posture. For Single-Leg Box Squat Negative, the slower lower makes compensations obvious and gives the coach time to adjust range, load, or support before pain or collapse.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Resilience / Slow Eccentric-Isometric Joint Resilience because the goal is tissue tolerance, joint ownership, and braking control after higher-priority speed, power, skill, or strength work.
cards.commonMisuse: Do not treat hard negatives as a daily warm-up or conditioning finisher. Avoid placing heavy hamstring, calf, groin, shoulder, elbow, or knee eccentrics before explosive Output when spring, speed, or skill quality matters.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by reducing range, tempo, load, lever length, instability, impact, or weekly frequency before accepting pain, shaking, breath holding, or position loss.

prerequisites: Pain-free basic pattern for Single-Leg Box Squat Negative
Ability to stop the set before alignment breaks
No acute flare-up in the target tissue or joint
movementRequirements.quality_threshold: Stop or regress if alignment, breathing, tempo, range control, or pain-free execution breaks down.

joint_resilience_profile.primary_strategy: Controlled eccentric / negative lowering in pain-free owned range.
joint_resilience_profile.range_emphasis: Use the largest range the athlete can own without compensation.
joint_resilience_profile.progression_trigger: Progress only when target tissue tolerates the dose with clean alignment and normal recovery within 24-48 hours.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Single-Leg Box Squat Negative is a slow negative/eccentric drill for single-leg squat eccentric capacity. It uses deliberate lowering, clean alignment, and enough rest to build control and tissue capacity without chasing fatigue.
setup: Stand in front of a box at a height that allows control without knee pain.
Choose a load, range, height, or lever length that allows a smooth negative without pain.
Set the athlete's start position before adding tempo or volume.
execution_steps: Create tension and start from a position the athlete can own.
Lower to the box for 5 seconds, sit or hover briefly, then use assistance to return if needed.
Keep breathing controlled and avoid bouncing, dropping, twisting, or rushing the last third of the range.
Reset each rep so the next negative starts from the same clean position.
athlete_cues: Move like you could pause anywhere.
Control the down, reset the up.
No pain, no collapse.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the rep if needed, then exhale slowly through the lowering phase.
Do not use breath holding to hide loss of position.
common_faults: Rushing the negative after the first half of the range.
Adding load before the athlete owns tempo and alignment.
Turning a tissue-control drill into a fatigue circuit.
Pushing through pain, joint pinch, or visible collapse.
quality_gate: Tempo stays within the prescribed range.
Target joint line stays clean with no uncontrolled wobble or collapse.
Athlete finishes the set with the same posture and range they started with.
No pain response during the set or unusual next-day flare-up.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, cramping that changes mechanics, numbness, dizziness, or joint pinch.
Tempo cannot be maintained for the planned range.
Breath holding, shaking, or compensation becomes the main strategy.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use hands-on support, a higher target, shorter range, and a 2-3 second lowering tempo. Keep the drill playful and stop well before shaking or pain.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use the same exercise with a modest tempo target, usually 3-4 seconds down. Progress range before load.
scaling.teen: Use after technical or output work, not as a fatigue finisher. Keep reps low and require repeatable alignment.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with the simplest setup and a pain-free range. Add load only after the athlete owns the slow lower and reset.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress by adding range, load, lever length, or tempo one variable at a time. Use longer rest and stop short of form failure.
scaling.older_adult: Prefer support, smaller range, lower load, and slower progressions. Prioritize confidence, breathing, and next-day recovery.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only symptom-free versions with clinician guidance when needed. Avoid breath holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, impact, and positions that provoke pelvic-floor, abdominal, or joint symptoms.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-specific adjustment. Individualize by training age, tissue history, joint symptoms, recovery response, pelvic-floor symptoms when relevant, footwear, surface, and fatigue state.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Seated Soleus Raise Eccentric is a slow negative/eccentric drill for soleus and Achilles bent-knee control. It uses deliberate lowering, clean alignment, and enough rest to build control and tissue capacity without chasing fatigue.
setup: Sit with knees bent and load across the thighs if appropriate.
Choose a load, range, height, or lever length that allows a smooth negative without pain.
Set the athlete's start position before adding tempo or volume.
execution_steps: Create tension and start from a position the athlete can own.
Lift the heels, then lower for 4-5 seconds while keeping pressure through the big toe and ball of foot.
Keep breathing controlled and avoid bouncing, dropping, twisting, or rushing the last third of the range.
Reset each rep so the next negative starts from the same clean position.
athlete_cues: Move like you could pause anywhere.
Control the down, reset the up.
No pain, no collapse.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the rep if needed, then exhale slowly through the lowering phase.
Do not use breath holding to hide loss of position.
common_faults: Rushing the negative after the first half of the range.
Adding load before the athlete owns tempo and alignment.
Turning a tissue-control drill into a fatigue circuit.
Pushing through pain, joint pinch, or visible collapse.
quality_gate: Tempo stays within the prescribed range.
Target joint line stays clean with no uncontrolled wobble or collapse.
Athlete finishes the set with the same posture and range they started with.
No pain response during the set or unusual next-day flare-up.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, cramping that changes mechanics, numbness, dizziness, or joint pinch.
Tempo cannot be maintained for the planned range.
Breath holding, shaking, or compensation becomes the main strategy.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use hands-on support, a higher target, shorter range, and a 2-3 second lowering tempo. Keep the drill playful and stop well before shaking or pain.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use the same exercise with a modest tempo target, usually 3-4 seconds down. Progress range before load.
scaling.teen: Use after technical or output work, not as a fatigue finisher. Keep reps low and require repeatable alignment.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with the simplest setup and a pain-free range. Add load only after the athlete owns the slow lower and reset.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress by adding range, load, lever length, or tempo one variable at a time. Use longer rest and stop short of form failure.
scaling.older_adult: Prefer support, smaller range, lower load, and slower progressions. Prioritize confidence, breathing, and next-day recovery.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only symptom-free versions with clinician guidance when needed. Avoid breath holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, impact, and positions that provoke pelvic-floor, abdominal, or joint symptoms.

cards.whyItWorks: Slow eccentrics create time under tension while the athlete must organize joints, breathing, and posture. For Eccentric Hollow Body Lower, the slower lower makes compensations obvious and gives the coach time to adjust range, load, or support before pain or collapse.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Resilience / Slow Eccentric-Isometric Joint Resilience because the goal is tissue tolerance, joint ownership, and braking control after higher-priority speed, power, skill, or strength work.
cards.commonMisuse: Do not treat hard negatives as a daily warm-up or conditioning finisher. Avoid placing heavy hamstring, calf, groin, shoulder, elbow, or knee eccentrics before explosive Output when spring, speed, or skill quality matters.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by reducing range, tempo, load, lever length, instability, impact, or weekly frequency before accepting pain, shaking, breath holding, or position loss.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Eccentric Hollow Body Lower is a slow negative/eccentric drill for anterior-core hollow-body eccentric control. It uses deliberate lowering, clean alignment, and enough rest to build control and tissue capacity without chasing fatigue.
setup: Lie on the back in a tucked hollow position with the low ribs pulled down.
Choose a load, range, height, or lever length that allows a smooth negative without pain.
Set the athlete's start position before adding tempo or volume.
execution_steps: Create tension and start from a position the athlete can own.
Slowly lengthen arms and legs for 4-5 seconds, stop before the low back changes, and reset.
Keep breathing controlled and avoid bouncing, dropping, twisting, or rushing the last third of the range.
Reset each rep so the next negative starts from the same clean position.
athlete_cues: Move like you could pause anywhere.
Control the down, reset the up.
No pain, no collapse.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the rep if needed, then exhale slowly through the lowering phase.
Do not use breath holding to hide loss of position.
common_faults: Rushing the negative after the first half of the range.
Adding load before the athlete owns tempo and alignment.
Turning a tissue-control drill into a fatigue circuit.
Pushing through pain, joint pinch, or visible collapse.
quality_gate: Tempo stays within the prescribed range.
Target joint line stays clean with no uncontrolled wobble or collapse.
Athlete finishes the set with the same posture and range they started with.
No pain response during the set or unusual next-day flare-up.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, cramping that changes mechanics, numbness, dizziness, or joint pinch.
Tempo cannot be maintained for the planned range.
Breath holding, shaking, or compensation becomes the main strategy.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use hands-on support, a higher target, shorter range, and a 2-3 second lowering tempo. Keep the drill playful and stop well before shaking or pain.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use the same exercise with a modest tempo target, usually 3-4 seconds down. Progress range before load.
scaling.teen: Use after technical or output work, not as a fatigue finisher. Keep reps low and require repeatable alignment.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with the simplest setup and a pain-free range. Add load only after the athlete owns the slow lower and reset.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress by adding range, load, lever length, or tempo one variable at a time. Use longer rest and stop short of form failure.
scaling.older_adult: Prefer support, smaller range, lower load, and slower progressions. Prioritize confidence, breathing, and next-day recovery.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only symptom-free versions with clinician guidance when needed. Avoid breath holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, impact, and positions that provoke pelvic-floor, abdominal, or joint symptoms.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Low Box Drop to Eccentric Landing Stick is a slow negative/eccentric drill for lower-body landing/braking control. It uses deliberate lowering, clean alignment, and enough rest to build control and tissue capacity without chasing fatigue.
setup: Stand on a low box with space to step down, not jump out aggressively.
Choose a load, range, height, or lever length that allows a smooth negative without pain.
Set the athlete's start position before adding tempo or volume.
execution_steps: Create tension and start from a position the athlete can own.
Step off, land quietly, sink into the landing for 1-2 seconds, and hold the stick for 2 seconds.

athlete_cues: Move like you could pause anywhere.
Control the down, reset the up.
No pain, no collapse.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the rep if needed, then exhale slowly through the lowering phase.
Do not use breath holding to hide loss of position.
common_faults: Rushing the negative after the first half of the range.
Adding load before the athlete owns tempo and alignment.
Turning a tissue-control drill into a fatigue circuit.
Pushing through pain, joint pinch, or visible collapse.
quality_gate: Tempo stays within the prescribed range.
Target joint line stays clean with no uncontrolled wobble or collapse.
Athlete finishes the set with the same posture and range they started with.

stop_signs: Sharp pain, cramping that changes mechanics, numbness, dizziness, or joint pinch.
Tempo cannot be maintained for the planned range.
Breath holding, shaking, or compensation becomes the main strategy.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Tempo Back Squat 5-Second Lower is a slow negative/eccentric drill for loaded squat eccentric capacity. It uses deliberate lowering, clean alignment, and enough rest to build control and tissue capacity without chasing fatigue.
setup: Set up in the rack with a conservative load and a consistent stance.
Choose a load, range, height, or lever length that allows a smooth negative without pain.
Set the athlete's start position before adding tempo or volume.
execution_steps: Create tension and start from a position the athlete can own.
Descend for 5 seconds, hit the planned depth without bouncing, and stand only if position stays clean.
Keep breathing controlled and avoid bouncing, dropping, twisting, or rushing the last third of the range.
Reset each rep so the next negative starts from the same clean position.
athlete_cues: Move like you could pause anywhere.
Control the down, reset the up.
No pain, no collapse.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the rep if needed, then exhale slowly through the lowering phase.
Do not use breath holding to hide loss of position.
common_faults: Rushing the negative after the first half of the range.
Adding load before the athlete owns tempo and alignment.
Turning a tissue-control drill into a fatigue circuit.
Pushing through pain, joint pinch, or visible collapse.
quality_gate: Tempo stays within the prescribed range.
Target joint line stays clean with no uncontrolled wobble or collapse.
Athlete finishes the set with the same posture and range they started with.
No pain response during the set or unusual next-day flare-up.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, cramping that changes mechanics, numbness, dizziness, or joint pinch.
Tempo cannot be maintained for the planned range.
Breath holding, shaking, or compensation becomes the main strategy.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use an assisted version, shorter lever, partial range, and fewer reps before attempting the full negative. Keep the drill playful and stop well before shaking or pain.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use the same exercise with a modest tempo target, usually 3-4 seconds down. Progress range before load.
scaling.teen: Use after technical or output work, not as a fatigue finisher. Keep reps low and require repeatable alignment.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with the simplest setup and a pain-free range. Add load only after the athlete owns the slow lower and reset.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress by adding range, load, lever length, or tempo one variable at a time. Use longer rest and stop short of form failure.
scaling.older_adult: Prefer support, smaller range, lower load, and slower progressions. Prioritize confidence, breathing, and next-day recovery.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only symptom-free versions with clinician guidance when needed. Avoid breath holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, impact, and positions that provoke pelvic-floor, abdominal, or joint symptoms.

cards.name: Slow Eccentric Hamstring Curl Machine
cards.cardSummary: Slow Eccentric Hamstring Curl Machine builds hamstring isolation eccentric capacity through a deliberate negative phase. Use it when the coach wants joint ownership, braking control, and local tissue capacity rather than speed or fatigue.
cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Resilience phase after Output and main Capacity work, or as a low-volume accessory after the main pattern. Harder eccentric tissue stress should not be placed before sprinting, jumping, cutting, or high-skill work.
cards.description: Slow Eccentric Hamstring Curl Machine is a slow negative/eccentric drill for hamstring isolation eccentric capacity. It uses deliberate lowering, clean alignment, and enough rest to build control and tissue capacity without chasing fatigue.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Slow Eccentric / Isometric Joint Resilience. Coach 5 seconds lowering, pain-free range, clean alignment, and enough rest to preserve tissue quality. Specific focus: hamstring isolation eccentric capacity.
cards.athleteLanguage: Move slow, own the lowering, and stop before pain or compensation.
cards.whyItWorks: Slow eccentrics create time under tension while the athlete must organize joints, breathing, and posture. For Slow Eccentric Hamstring Curl Machine, the slower lower makes compensations obvious and gives the coach time to adjust range, load, or support before pain or collapse.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Resilience / Slow Eccentric-Isometric Joint Resilience because the goal is tissue tolerance, joint ownership, and braking control after higher-priority speed, power, skill, or strength work.
cards.commonMisuse: Do not treat hard negatives as a daily warm-up or conditioning finisher. Avoid placing heavy hamstring, calf, groin, shoulder, elbow, or knee eccentrics before explosive Output when spring, speed, or skill quality matters.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by reducing range, tempo, load, lever length, instability, impact, or weekly frequency before accepting pain, shaking, breath holding, or position loss.

prerequisites: Pain-free basic pattern for Slow Eccentric Hamstring Curl Machine
Ability to stop the set before alignment breaks
No acute flare-up in the target tissue or joint
movementRequirements.quality_threshold: Stop or regress if alignment, breathing, tempo, range control, or pain-free execution breaks down.

joint_resilience_profile.primary_strategy: Controlled eccentric / negative lowering in pain-free owned range.
joint_resilience_profile.range_emphasis: Use the largest range the athlete can own without compensation.
joint_resilience_profile.progression_trigger: Progress only when target tissue tolerates the dose with clean alignment and normal recovery within 24-48 hours.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Slow Eccentric Hamstring Curl Machine is a slow negative/eccentric drill for hamstring isolation eccentric capacity. It uses deliberate lowering, clean alignment, and enough rest to build control and tissue capacity without chasing fatigue.
setup: Set the machine so the knee joint aligns with the pivot and the hips stay stable.
Choose a load, range, height, or lever length that allows a smooth negative without pain.
Set the athlete's start position before adding tempo or volume.
execution_steps: Create tension and start from a position the athlete can own.
Curl up with control, then lower for 5 seconds without lifting the hips or rushing the last third.
Keep breathing controlled and avoid bouncing, dropping, twisting, or rushing the last third of the range.
Reset each rep so the next negative starts from the same clean position.
athlete_cues: Move like you could pause anywhere.
Control the down, reset the up.
No pain, no collapse.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the rep if needed, then exhale slowly through the lowering phase.
Do not use breath holding to hide loss of position.
common_faults: Rushing the negative after the first half of the range.
Adding load before the athlete owns tempo and alignment.
Turning a tissue-control drill into a fatigue circuit.
Pushing through pain, joint pinch, or visible collapse.
quality_gate: Tempo stays within the prescribed range.
Target joint line stays clean with no uncontrolled wobble or collapse.
Athlete finishes the set with the same posture and range they started with.
No pain response during the set or unusual next-day flare-up.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, cramping that changes mechanics, numbness, dizziness, or joint pinch.
Tempo cannot be maintained for the planned range.
Breath holding, shaking, or compensation becomes the main strategy.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use hands-on support, a higher target, shorter range, and a 2-3 second lowering tempo. Keep the drill playful and stop well before shaking or pain.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use the same exercise with a modest tempo target, usually 3-4 seconds down. Progress range before load.
scaling.teen: Use after technical or output work, not as a fatigue finisher. Keep reps low and require repeatable alignment.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with the simplest setup and a pain-free range. Add load only after the athlete owns the slow lower and reset.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress by adding range, load, lever length, or tempo one variable at a time. Use longer rest and stop short of form failure.
scaling.older_adult: Prefer support, smaller range, lower load, and slower progressions. Prioritize confidence, breathing, and next-day recovery.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only symptom-free versions with clinician guidance when needed. Avoid breath holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, impact, and positions that provoke pelvic-floor, abdominal, or joint symptoms.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-specific adjustment. Individualize by training age, tissue history, joint symptoms, recovery response, pelvic-floor symptoms when relevant, footwear, surface, and fatigue state.

Athlete can maintain controlled breathing and alignment during a slow lower.
Target tissue is not acutely sore, irritated, or recently strained.
contraindications: Sharp pain, joint instability, acute tissue injury, or neurologic symptoms.
Uncontrolled range or inability to safely exit the negative.
Recent high-volume eccentric exposure to the same tissue without recovery.

Supported isometric hold at a comfortable joint angle
Lighter load or shorter lever version

Choose a load, range, height, or lever length that allows a smooth negative without pain.
